prompt: A witty cartoon educational illustration with clean lines and a warm palette: a small explorer stands at the edge of a path that fans out ahead into a widening cone of soft fog, narrow and clear near the feet but spreading into a broad fan of pale ghostly branching trails toward the horizon, the explorer calmly holding a lantern instead of demanding a single road. Style: friendly XKCD or Kurzgesagt panel, expressive posture, one clear visual idea, generous negative space, textbook suitable. No text, letters, numbers, or labels anywhere in the image.
alt: An explorer at the mouth of a fan shaped cone of fog that narrows near the feet and widens into many faint branching trails toward the horizon, holding a lantern instead of insisting on one road.
caption: A forecast is not a single road into the future but a widening cone of possibilities, and the honest job is to map the whole spread rather than guess one path.

after_heading: 2. Sources of Uncertainty: Aleatoric vs Epistemic
prompt: A witty cartoon educational illustration with clean lines and a warm palette: split scene with two characters. On the left a scientist calmly watches tumbling dice in a clear glass bowl, the randomness is permanent and accepted with a shrug. On the right the same scientist peers through thinning fog around a half hidden shape, lifting a magnifying glass, the fog visibly clearing as more footprints accumulate. Style: clean XKCD or Kurzgesagt panel, expressive faces, two panel symmetry, generous negative space, textbook suitable. No text, letters, numbers, or labels anywhere in the image.
alt: A two part scene contrasting permanent dice randomness in a glass bowl with a foggy shape that grows clearer as a scientist gathers more footprints and peers through a magnifying glass.
caption: Aleatoric uncertainty is the irreducible roll of the dice that more data cannot remove, while epistemic uncertainty is fog around the truth that thins as evidence accumulates.

prompt: A witty cartoon educational illustration with clean lines and a warm palette: two weather forecasters side by side. One holds a wide floppy umbrella spread so enormously that rain never catches anyone but the prediction is uselessly vague, looking smug yet unhelpful. The other holds a slim precise umbrella positioned exactly over a single raindrop about to fall, confident and tight, both standing under the same drizzly cloud. Style: clean XKCD or Kurzgesagt panel, expressive faces, clear contrast of wide versus tight, generous negative space, textbook suitable. No text, letters, numbers, or labels anywhere in the image.
alt: Two forecasters under the same cloud, one hiding behind an absurdly huge vague umbrella that is always technically right, the other holding a slim precise umbrella aimed exactly at the falling raindrop.
caption: A forecaster who hedges with enormous intervals is calibrated but useless, so the goal is sharpness subject to calibration: the tightest honest prediction, not the safest vague one.

prompt: A witty cartoon educational illustration with clean lines and a warm palette: a baker stands before a tall soft mound of dough shaped like a smooth hill, slicing it into several horizontal layers at chosen heights with a long knife, each cut marking a level of the pile rather than its center. The baker eyes the cut heights deliberately, not slicing through the middle but at picked fractions up the slope. Style: clean XKCD or Kurzgesagt panel, expressive concentration, clear stacked horizontal cuts, generous negative space, textbook suitable. No text, letters, numbers, or labels anywhere in the image.
alt: A baker deliberately slicing a smooth dough hill into horizontal layers at several chosen heights up the slope rather than cutting straight through the middle.
caption: Quantile regression does not predict the average outcome but carves the distribution at chosen levels, learning each slice height directly from an asymmetric pinball penalty.

after_heading: 1. The Conformal Idea: A Coverage Guarantee Around Any Forecaster
prompt: A witty cartoon educational illustration with clean lines and a warm palette: a tailor wraps a snug protective band around a wildly assorted lineup of odd objects, a clockwork robot, a crystal ball, a tiny abacus, fitting each with a band sized from the same stack of past measurements, indifferent to what each object is inside. The tailor works confidently with a measuring ribbon, treating every black box the same. Style: clean XKCD or Kurzgesagt panel, expressive calm competence, row of varied objects each getting a fitted band, generous negative space, textbook suitable. No text, letters, numbers, or labels anywhere in the image.
alt: A tailor fitting a snug protective band around a row of completely different black box objects using the same stack of past measurements, indifferent to what each one is inside.
caption: Conformal prediction wraps any forecaster, no matter how strange its internals, in an interval whose coverage is guaranteed from past errors alone.

after_heading: 2. The Time-Series Problem: Exchangeability Is Violated
prompt: A witty cartoon educational illustration with clean lines and a warm palette: a confident gambler shuffles a deck of cards that are clearly chained together in a fixed temporal order, the chain links visibly straining as he tries to shuffle, while behind him a calendar arrow marches forward and a banded interval around his prediction quietly shrinks far too narrow. He grins, unaware the cards refuse to be shuffled. Style: clean XKCD or Kurzgesagt panel, ironic overconfidence, visible chain linking the cards, generous negative space, textbook suitable. No text, letters, numbers, or labels anywhere in the image.
alt: An overconfident gambler trying to shuffle a deck of cards that are physically chained in temporal order, while his prediction band shrinks unrealistically narrow and a forward arrow marches on behind him.
caption: Time ordered data cannot be shuffled like an exchangeable deck, so naive conformal intervals quietly undercover, which is why adaptive variants are needed for forecasting.
